descriptions.description: tokens that are code identifiers or placeholder names (capitalized/underscored identifiers like NAME_*, function/class names and other programming symbols).
The neuron activates on numeric tokens and variable‐style placeholders (e.g. digits like “1”, “7–9”, and markers like “NAME_”), i.e. it’s looking for numbers or parameter placeholders in the text.
The neuron activates on mentions of chemical compounds and formulas, especially titanium dioxide (e.g. “titanium dioxide,” “Ti,” “O,” “2”).
terms related to optical telecommunications and signal processing.
The neuron selectively detects technical scientific terminology—especially specialized nouns naming measured properties, materials, or domain‐specific devices.
places where the assistant begins a reply or introduces/generated code/structured output (e.g., "Here is", code block markers and SQL/JSON output).
This neuron activates on the main article title or subject heading—i.e. the first proper-name tokens that label what the document is about.
The neuron fires on mentions of percentages (e.g. “%” or the word “percent”/“percentual” and equivalents).
This neuron specifically fires on the intensifying adjective “tremendous.”
The neuron fires on HTML list markup, especially tokens of ordered‐list and list‐item tags (e.g. “<ol”, its start‐attribute, and “li”).
This neuron is essentially inactive—it never reliably activates for any token or pattern.
